0:{"P":null,"c":["","observation","void-in-fashion"],"q":"","i":false,"f":[[["",{"children":["observation",{"children":[["slug","void-in-fashion","d",null],{"children":["__PAGE__",{}]}]}]},"$undefined","$undefined",16],[["$","$1","c",{"children":[[["$","link","0",{"rel":"stylesheet","href":"/_next/static/chunks/0-f4fumw51mqk.css","precedence":"next","crossOrigin":"$undefined","nonce":"$undefined"}],["$","script","script-0",{"src":"/_next/static/chunks/17mgjxqmeuvto.js","async":true,"nonce":"$undefined"}],["$","script","script-1",{"src":"/_next/static/chunks/0d3shmwh5_nmn.js","async":true,"nonce":"$undefined"}]],["$","html",null,{"lang":"zh-CN","suppressHydrationWarning":true,"children":[["$","head",null,{"children":[["$","link",null,{"rel":"manifest","href":"/manifest.json"}],["$","meta",null,{"name":"google-site-verification","content":"2vOV2uOeIqVVUWdP7CR-IlQS9Q8xroNrzhLKbakOWrA"}],["$","meta",null,{"name":"robots","content":"index, d:"$W13"
14:I[88589,["/_next/static/chunks/17mgjxqmeuvto.js","/_next/static/chunks/0d3shmwh5_nmn.js","/_next/static/chunks/0ant411rewcrv.js"],"default"]
15:I[22016,["/_next/static/chunks/17mgjxqmeuvto.js","/_next/static/chunks/0d3shmwh5_nmn.js","/_next/static/chunks/0ant411rewcrv.js"],""]
16:Te0a,<p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="0">
There is a Japanese concept called *ma*—the negative space, the gap, the pause between notes. In fashion, this translates to the void: the absence that defines presence.</p><h2 class="text-2xl font-light mt-16 mb-8" key="1">The Architecture of Empty Space</h2><p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="2">Rei Kawakubo's Spring 2026 collection for Comme des Garçons featured garments with literal holes—strategic absences where fabric should be. Critics called it deconstruction. But it was something more precise: an assertion that clothing need not cover to clothe.</p><p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="3">This is 虚 (xu)—the void, the empty, the receptive. In Daoist thought, the empty vessel is what makes the vessel useful. It is not the clay of the cup that holds the tea, but the space within.</p><p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="4">Similarly, it is not the fabric that makes the garment wearable, but the space it creates around the body.</p><h2 class="text-2xl font-light mt-16 mb-8" key="5">The Cut-Out as Philosophy</h2><p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="6">Consider the evolution of the cut-out:</p><ul class="list-disc list-inside space-y-2 mb-6 text-[#8a8a8a]" key="7"><li>**1980s**: Strategic exposure for sex appeal (Versace)</li><li>**2000s**: Deconstructive statement (Margiela)</li><li>**2026**: Spatial definition (Kawakubo, Botter, Y/Project)</li></ul><p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="8">The contemporary cut-out is not about showing skin. It is about defining volume through absence. The hole becomes a frame, directing the eye not to what is exposed, but to the silhouette that remains.</p><h2 class="text-2xl font-light mt-16 mb-8" key="9">Negative Space in Daily Dressing</h2><p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="10">This philosophy extends beyond runway theatrics. The oversized blazer with sleeves too long. The dress with an undefined waist. The trousers that pool at the ankle.</p><p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="11">These are all assertions of void—garments that refuse to conform to the body, instead creating space around it. The wearer inhabits the clothing; the clothing does not inhabit the wearer.</p><h2 class="text-2xl font-light mt-16 mb-8" key="12">The Dao of Dressing</h2><p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="13">老子 wrote: "Thirty spokes share the wheel's hub; it is the center hole that makes it useful."</p><p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="14">The garment's value lies partly in what it does not do. It does not constrain. It does not define. It creates room—physical, visual, conceptual—for the wearer to exist.</p><p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="15">In an era of body-conscious dressing, of seams that bind and fabrics that compress, this approach feels radical. The garment as shelter, not definition. The void as protection.</p><h2 class="text-2xl font-light mt-16 mb-8" key="16">Looking Forward</h2><p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="17">We are seeing this philosophy extend to retail spaces, to packaging, to the entire ecosystem of fashion. The empty hanger. The unprinted box. The store with more air than merchandise.</p><p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="18">Each absence is a statement. Each void is a presence.</p><hr class="border-t border-[#e5e5e5] my-12" key="19" /><p class="text-[#8a8a8a] leading-loose mb-6" key="20">*Observation from the Spring/Summer 2026 collections.*
    </p>9:["$","main",null,{"className":"min-h-screen","children":[["$","$L14",null,{}],["$","article",null,{"className":"pt-32 pb-20 px-6","children":["$","div",null,{"className":"max-w-3xl mx-auto","children":[["$","$L15",null,{"href":"/","className":"text-sm tracking-widest uppercase text-[#8a8a8a] hover:text-[#1a1a1a] transition-colors mb-12 inline-block","children":"← Back"}],["$","header",null,{"className":"mb-16","children":[["$","div",null,{"className":"flex items-center space-x-4 mb-6","children":[["$","span",null,{"className":"text-xs tracking-[0.2em] uppercase text-[#c9a961]","children":"void"}],["$","span",null,{"className":"text-xs text-[#8a8a8a]","children":["2026.04.05"," · ",7," min read"]}]]}],["$","h1",null,{"className":"text-4xl md:text-5xl font-light leading-tight mb-8","children":"The Void: Presence in Absence"}],["$","p",null,{"className":"text-xl text-[#8a8a8a] leading-relaxed max-w-2xl","children":"Exploring negative space in garment design. When what is removed becomes more powerful than what remains."}]]}],["$","div",null,{"className":"w-px h-16 bg-gradient-to-b from-[#1a1a1a] to-transparent mx-auto mb-16"}],["$","div",null,{"className":"prose prose-lg max-w-none","dangerouslySetInnerHTML":{"__html":"$16"}}],"$L17"]}]}],"$L18"]}]
